title: Making leaf mould from autumn leaves
subtitle: A 12 to 18 month fungal composting process that converts fallen leaves into a free soil conditioner and mulch
excerpt: Leaf mould is made by stacking autumn leaves in a wire cage or black bin bags and leaving them for 12 to 24 months. The decomposition is entirely fungal, requires no turning, and converts even shredded oak leaves into a crumbly, water-retentive soil conditioner. The result is roughly equivalent to peat in texture and water-holding capacity but costs nothing.
sourceNotes: RHS guidance on leaf mould making and use. Garden Organic leaf mould fact sheet. BSI PAS 100:2018 on composted materials quality.

glossaryTerms.definition: Partially decomposed leaves broken down primarily by fungal activity over 12 to 24 months. Used as a mulch, soil conditioner, or component of seed-raising compost.
Slow, unmanaged composting that proceeds at ambient temperatures, typically taking 12 to 24 months to produce finished compost.

projectSchedule.title: Collect and stack leaves
projectSchedule.body: Gather autumn leaves from late October through November. Stack in a wire cage or fill black bags. Wet thoroughly if leaves are dry.

projectSchedule.title: Six-month check
projectSchedule.body: Inspect the cage or bags. Leaves should have darkened and reduced in volume by half. If dry, water. If very compacted, aerate with a fork.

projectSchedule.title: 12-month leaf mould ready for mulching
projectSchedule.body: After 12 months, the outer layer is crumbly dark leaf mould suitable for mulching. The inner core may need another 6 months. Use the outer material and leave the core to continue.

projectSchedule.title: 18-month fine leaf mould ready for compost
projectSchedule.body: After 18 months, the entire batch is usually fine enough to sieve for seed-raising compost. Screen through a 5 mm mesh.

content.text: One large garden tree can drop 100 to 200 kg of leaves in autumn. Sent to the green waste bin, they become municipal compost. Stacked in a cage and left for a year, they become
leaf mould
: a free, high-quality soil conditioner that retains 4 to 5 times its dry weight in water and suppresses annual weeds as a 50 to 75 mm mulch. The process is entirely
cold composting
driven by fungi rather than bacteria: it requires no turning, no monitoring, and no management beyond stacking and waiting.
What you will need
Wire mesh (chicken wire, 25 mm mesh) to form a cage at least 1 m x 1 m x 1 m. A smaller cage works but takes longer because the leaf mass dries out faster at small volumes.
Four wooden posts (50 x 50 mm, 1.2 m long) to support the cage.
Alternatively, use heavy-duty black plastic bags with a few 10 mm holes in the sides and base. This works in smaller gardens or where a cage is not practical.
Building and filling the cage
Drive the four posts into a shaded or partially shaded spot (full sun dries the pile too fast). A spot near the compost heap or against a fence is ideal.
Wrap the wire mesh around the posts and fasten it with wire ties at 300 mm intervals. The cage does not need a base or a top.

Collect leaves from October through November. Wet leaves can be stacked directly. Dry leaves benefit from moistening with a hosepipe as you fill the cage: they need to be damp (not waterlogged) to start fungal decomposition. Aim for the moisture level of a wrung-out sponge.

Shredded leaves decompose in 6 to 12 months; whole oak, beech, or sycamore leaves take 18 to 24 months. Shredding with a lawn mower (running over raked piles on the lawn) or a garden shredder accelerates the process significantly.
Uses for leaf mould at different stages
12 months (coarse, fibrous): spread as a 50 to 75 mm mulch around fruit trees, shrubs, and perennials. Suppresses annual weeds and retains moisture.
18 to 24 months (fine and crumbly): sieve through 5 mm mesh and mix at 50 percent with sharp sand for a seed-raising compost. Mix at 30 percent with topsoil for potting mix.
Any stage: add to the main compost heap as a carbon layer (browns) between nitrogen-rich greens.
The pile is probably too dry. Water thoroughly and re-check after one month. In a very dry summer, the pile may need watering monthly.

attrs.symptom: White fungal growth visible in the pile

content.text: This is the active decomposer working. White fungal mycelium in a leaf pile is normal and beneficial, not a problem.
